metadata.title: Docker+Prefect Observability Hardening — Zero-Blind-Spot Training Monitoring
metadata.priority: P0 — blocks next factorial pass (won't launch until observability is right)

root-causes.cause: torch.cuda.is_available() returned False due to CUDA 13.0 vs driver 560.35.05 mismatch. PyTorch silently fell back to CPU. train_flow.py line 461: device_str = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu" — no guard. Training ran for 4 hours on CPU without ANY error or warning. FIX: Fail immediately when CUDA unavailable in GPU flows.

Training produced only 47 log lines in 4 hours. Python output buffering + no forced flush + no structured progress = total blindness. Docker logs useless. MLflow had system metrics but zero training metrics. FIX: Epoch-level JSONL progress with force-flush, visible in Docker logs.

GPU utilization at 0% was misinterpreted as "data loading." No periodic GPU heartbeat to detect training-not-actually-using-GPU. The only diagnostic was manual `docker exec` into the container after 4 hours. FIX: Periodic GPU heartbeat — alert when util < threshold for > N minutes.

No mechanism to detect "container running but not producing output." Docker healthcheck only checks container alive (basic process check). MLflow has no stall alert — it only records what flows log. FIX: Heartbeat file + health check + stall detection.

cause.title: CUDA version mismatch undetected at startup

root-causes.cause: Container's PyTorch was compiled with CUDA 13.0 but host driver only supports CUDA 12.6. No preflight check caught this. The mismatch is detectable at container startup by comparing torch.version.cuda vs nvidia-smi driver version. FIX: CUDA version preflight in training flow startup.

phase.title: CUDA Availability Guard
phase.description:
Add a require_cuda_context() guard function that GPU flows call at startup, analogous to the existing require_docker_context(). When CUDA is unavailable, the flow MUST fail immediately with a diagnostic error message showing: - torch.cuda.is_available() result - torch.version.cuda (PyTorch CUDA compilation version) - NVIDIA driver version (from nvidia-smi or pynvml) - Actionable fix suggestion
This guard is called at the TOP of training_flow() and hpo_flow() — BEFORE any model creation, data loading, or heavy computation.
Escape hatch: MINIVESS_ALLOW_CPU=1 env var for pytest only (like ALLOW_HOST).

task.title: Create src/minivess/orchestration/cuda_guard.py
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that require_cuda_context("train") raises RuntimeError when torch.cuda.is_available() returns False (mock torch.cuda.is_available). Test that the error message contains diagnostic info (driver version, PyTorch CUDA version). Test that MINIVESS_ALLOW_CPU=1 bypasses the guard. Test that the function is a no-op when CUDA IS available.

task.title: Integrate guard into train_flow.py
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that training_flow() calls require_cuda_context() before train_one_fold_task(). Mock the guard. Verify it raises when CUDA unavailable. Verify training never starts (no MLflow run created).

task.title: Add CUDA version mismatch detection (RC5)
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test detect_cuda_version_mismatch() that compares container PyTorch CUDA version (torch.version.cuda) vs host driver capability. When mismatch detected, return a CudaMismatchResult with details. Test with mocked version strings (e.g., PyTorch CUDA "13.0" vs driver "12.6"). Test that warnings are logged but the function does not raise (advisory, since the guard itself catches the unavailability).

phase.success-criteria: - require_cuda_context() raises RuntimeError within 1 second when CUDA unavailable - Error message includes PyTorch CUDA version, driver info, and actionable fix - MINIVESS_ALLOW_CPU=1 bypasses the guard (test-only escape hatch) - All tests pass in make test-staging (no model_loading, no slow) - Existing train_flow tests still pass (guard mocked or ALLOW_CPU=1 set)

phase.title: GPU Heartbeat and Utilization Monitor
phase.description:
Create a background monitor that periodically checks GPU utilization and logs a heartbeat. If GPU utilization stays below a configurable threshold (default 5%) for longer than a configurable duration (default 2 minutes), log an ERROR-level alert.
This catches: - Training silently running on CPU (GPU util = 0%) - Training stalled in data loading indefinitely - GPU process crash while container keeps running
Implementation: A lightweight background thread using pynvml (already a transitive dep of PyTorch). Writes heartbeat.json to LOGS_DIR for external monitoring. Thresholds come from .env.example (new vars).
NOTE: The existing GpuMonitor in pipeline/trainer.py collects per-epoch snapshots but does NOT run as a background thread between epochs. This new component runs continuously and detects inter-epoch stalls.

task.title: Create src/minivess/observability/gpu_heartbeat.py
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test GpuHeartbeatMonitor with mocked pynvml that: (a) starts a daemon thread on enter, stops on exit (context manager) (b) writes heartbeat.json to output_dir every check_interval_s seconds (c) logs ERROR when gpu_util_pct < threshold for > alert_after_s seconds (d) heartbeat.json contains: timestamp, gpu_util_pct, gpu_memory_used_mb, gpu_temp_c, status ("healthy"|"low_utilization"|"no_gpu") (e) graceful no-op when pynvml unavailable (CPU-only environments) (f) thread cleanup: verify is_alive() == False after __exit__

task.title: Add .env.example variables for heartbeat config
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that .env.example contains GPU_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_S, GPU_HEARTBEAT_LOW_UTIL_THRESHOLD_PCT, GPU_HEARTBEAT_ALERT_AFTER_S. Verify defaults are sensible (30s interval, 5% threshold, 120s alert). Use existing test_env_single_source.py pattern for enforcement.
task.test-file: tests/v2/unit/test_env_single_source.py (extended — add new vars to expected set)

task.title: Integrate heartbeat into train_flow.py
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that training_flow() creates GpuHeartbeatMonitor as a context manager around the fold training loop. Verify heartbeat.json is written to LOGS_DIR. Mock the monitor to verify lifecycle (start/stop).

phase.success-criteria: - Background thread starts before training, stops after training - heartbeat.json updated every GPU_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_S seconds - ERROR logged when GPU util below threshold for alert_after_s - Zero overhead when pynvml unavailable (graceful degradation) - Thread is daemon (won't prevent container exit) - All tests in make test-staging (mocked pynvml, no GPU required)

phase.title: Structured Epoch Progress with Force-Flush
phase.description:
Enhance the existing StructuredEventLogger to emit epoch-level progress that is: (a) Machine-parseable (JSONL to events.jsonl — already implemented) (b) Human-readable (formatted progress line to Python logger — partially exists) (c) Force-flushed after every epoch (sys.stdout.flush() + sys.stderr.flush()) (d) Visible in Docker logs (already works via PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 in base images) (e) Visible in Prefect UI (already works via PREFECT_LOGGING_EXTRA_LOGGERS)
The existing trainer.py already logs epoch progress at line 819-827, but: - It does NOT force-flush stdout/stderr - It does NOT update heartbeat.json - It does NOT emit structured JSONL events per epoch
This phase wires the trainer's epoch completion into StructuredEventLogger for durable JSONL events AND heartbeat updates. Also adds sys.stdout.flush() after each epoch log line to ensure Docker log visibility.
NOTE: PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 is already set in all 3 base Dockerfiles. This phase ensures the remaining gap (explicit flush in critical paths) is closed.

task.title: Add epoch event methods to StructuredEventLogger
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test log_epoch_complete() method that: (a) writes JSONL event with: epoch, max_epochs, train_loss, val_loss, val_dice, lr, epoch_wall_s, gpu_util_pct, vram_used_mb (b) updates heartbeat.json with same data + ETA + status (c) ETA computation: remaining_epochs * avg_epoch_time Test log_training_start() and log_training_end() for run-level events.

task.title: Wire StructuredEventLogger into SegmentationTrainer.fit()
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that SegmentationTrainer accepts an optional StructuredEventLogger parameter. When provided, fit() calls log_epoch_complete() after each epoch. When not provided, behavior unchanged (backward compatible). Also test that sys.stdout.flush() + sys.stderr.flush() are called after each epoch's log line. Test with mock logger: verify correct epoch/loss/lr values passed.

task.title: Wire StructuredEventLogger into train_flow.py
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that training_flow() creates StructuredEventLogger(output_dir=LOGS_DIR) and passes it to SegmentationTrainer. Verify LOGS_DIR/events.jsonl is created. Verify LOGS_DIR/heartbeat.json is updated. Mock trainer to verify wiring.

phase.success-criteria: - Every epoch produces a JSONL event line in events.jsonl - heartbeat.json updated after every epoch with ETA - sys.stdout.flush() called after every epoch log line - Progress visible in: Docker logs, Prefect UI, events.jsonl, heartbeat.json - Backward compatible: trainer works without StructuredEventLogger - All tests in make test-staging (no model loading)

phase.title: Training Stall Detection via Heartbeat Staleness
phase.description:
Build on the heartbeat.json from Phases 2 and 3 to detect training stalls. A stall is defined as: heartbeat.json not updated for > STALL_THRESHOLD_MINUTES.
Three detection layers: 1. In-process: The GpuHeartbeatMonitor (Phase 2) checks if the TRAINING heartbeat has been updated. If not → log ERROR (training thread may be hung). 2. Docker healthcheck: A lightweight health check script that reads heartbeat.json and returns unhealthy when stale. This makes `docker ps` show the container as unhealthy. 3. External: is_heartbeat_stale() (already exists in train_flow.py line 144) can be called by monitoring scripts or Prefect automations.
This phase implements layer 2 (Docker healthcheck) and extends layer 1.

task.title: Create scripts/healthcheck_training.py
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test the health check script that: (a) reads heartbeat.json from LOGS_DIR environment variable (b) returns exit 0 if heartbeat is fresh (< stall threshold) (c) returns exit 1 if heartbeat is stale (>= stall threshold) (d) returns exit 1 if heartbeat.json does not exist AND container has been running for > grace_period_minutes (default 10) (e) returns exit 0 during grace period (startup phase) (f) threshold and grace period from env vars (STALL_THRESHOLD_MINUTES, HEALTH_GRACE_PERIOD_MINUTES)

task.title: Add stall detection to GpuHeartbeatMonitor
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that GpuHeartbeatMonitor reads the training heartbeat.json (written by StructuredEventLogger) and detects when it becomes stale. When stale for > alert_after_s: log ERROR "Training appears stalled — no epoch progress in {minutes} minutes. Last heartbeat: {timestamp}". Test with mocked file timestamps.

task.title: Add .env.example variables for stall detection
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that .env.example contains STALL_THRESHOLD_MINUTES and HEALTH_GRACE_PERIOD_MINUTES with sensible defaults (30 min, 10 min).

phase.success-criteria: - healthcheck_training.py exits 0 for fresh heartbeat, 1 for stale - Grace period prevents false alarms during container startup - GpuHeartbeatMonitor logs ERROR when training heartbeat stale - healthcheck script usable as Docker HEALTHCHECK CMD - All tests in make test-staging

phase.title: Prefect Task-Level Timing and Visibility
phase.description:
Add a timing decorator/context manager that wraps every Prefect @task to emit structured start/end events with duration. This makes Prefect UI show: - Task name + start timestamp - Task name + end timestamp + duration + status (completed/failed) - Overall flow timing summary
The existing StructuredLogger class (structured_logging.py) already supports task_start/task_end events but is NOT wired into any actual Prefect tasks. This phase wires it in.
Implementation: A Prefect task hook (on_completion, on_failure) that automatically emits events, OR a thin decorator that wraps @task functions. Prefect 3.x supports task hooks natively — library-first approach.

task.title: Create Prefect task timing hooks
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test create_task_timing_hooks() that returns (on_completion, on_failure) callables compatible with Prefect 3.x task hook signature. on_completion logs: task_name, duration_ms, status="completed" on_failure logs: task_name, duration_ms, status="failed", error_type Both emit StructuredLogger events. Test with mock Prefect task context (TaskRunContext).

task.title: Apply timing hooks to train_flow.py tasks
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that all @task-decorated functions in train_flow.py include the timing hooks (on_completion, on_failure). Verify via AST inspection that every @task decorator has hooks kwarg. Test that the hooks emit events when tasks complete.

task.title: Apply timing hooks to other GPU flows
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that @task decorators in hpo_flow.py, analysis_flow.py, and post_training_flow.py also include timing hooks. AST-based test scans all flow files to enforce consistency.

phase.success-criteria: - Every @task in GPU flows has on_completion + on_failure hooks - Hooks emit structured JSONL events (task_start, task_end) - Task duration visible in Prefect UI via structured logger - AST-based test ensures no @task escapes without hooks - All tests in make test-staging (no Prefect server required)

phase.title: Docker Compose Health Checks for Flow Containers
phase.description:
Add health checks to GPU flow services in docker-compose.flows.yml that detect "container running but not producing output."
Current state: The infra compose (docker-compose.yml) has health checks for postgres, minio, mlflow, etc. The flows compose (docker-compose.flows.yml) has ZERO health checks on flow containers.
NOTE: Per CLAUDE.md Rule #31, we do NOT modify YAML config files without explicit user authorization. This phase PREPARES the health check script (Phase 4.1 already created it) and DOCUMENTS the compose YAML additions the user can apply. The user will add the healthcheck stanzas themselves.
The health check uses scripts/healthcheck_training.py from Phase 4.

task.title: Document compose healthcheck stanzas
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that the documentation file exists and contains valid Docker Compose healthcheck YAML snippets for train, hpo, and post_training services. Validate the YAML syntax.

task.title: Verify healthcheck script is in Docker image
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that Dockerfile.train COPYs scripts/ directory (already does at line 14: COPY --chown=minivess:minivess scripts/ scripts/) and that healthcheck_training.py is importable from within the container. Verify the script uses only stdlib (no extra deps needed).

phase.success-criteria: - healthcheck_training.py uses only stdlib (no import of minivess package) - Documentation provides copy-paste YAML stanzas for train/hpo/post_training - Stanzas use the correct script path (scripts/healthcheck_training.py) - User can add healthchecks by copying documented YAML into compose file

phase.title: Container Resource Monitoring via Prometheus Metrics
phase.description:
Extend the existing prometheus_metrics.py to expose container-level resource gauges: CPU percent, memory RSS, GPU utilization, GPU memory, GPU temperature. These are readable by Grafana dashboards and the Prefect CLI.
The existing prometheus_metrics.py already defines cost gauges. This phase adds resource gauges that the GpuHeartbeatMonitor (Phase 2) updates periodically.
Data flow: GpuHeartbeatMonitor → update_resource_gauges() → Prometheus Gauges → Grafana dashboard scrapes /metrics endpoint → Prefect UI shows resource utilization
This provides the "container resource monitoring visible in Prefect UI or CLI" requirement from the incident remediation list.

task.title: Add resource Prometheus gauges
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that prometheus_metrics.py defines new gauges: minivess_container_cpu_percent, minivess_container_memory_rss_gb, minivess_container_gpu_utilization_pct, minivess_container_gpu_memory_used_mb, minivess_container_gpu_temperature_c. Test update_resource_gauges(snapshot) sets all gauges correctly. Test with mock snapshot dict.

task.title: Wire GpuHeartbeatMonitor to Prometheus gauges
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that GpuHeartbeatMonitor calls update_resource_gauges() on each heartbeat tick. Verify gauges are updated with mock pynvml data. Test that Prometheus registry contains the expected gauge values after a heartbeat cycle.

phase.success-criteria: - 5 new Prometheus gauges registered with minivess_container_ prefix - Gauges updated every heartbeat interval - Gauges readable by Grafana via /metrics endpoint - Zero overhead when Prometheus client not available (graceful import) - All tests in make test-staging

phase.title: MLflow Training Stall Alert
phase.description:
Add a check that detects when a training run has not logged any new MLflow metrics for longer than a configurable threshold. This is the MLflow-side complement to the heartbeat-based stall detection in Phase 4.
This catches the scenario where the container IS healthy (heartbeat updating) but training has stopped producing MLflow metrics (e.g., stuck in a MONAI DataLoader deadlock, or model forward pass hanging).
Implementation: A function that queries the latest metric timestamp from MLflow for a given run_id and compares it to now(). If stale, log an alert and tag the run with "stall_detected=true".
This is designed to be called periodically by the GpuHeartbeatMonitor or by an external monitoring script (e.g., the /factorial-monitor skill).

task.title: Create MLflow stall detection function
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test detect_mlflow_metric_stall(run_id, threshold_minutes) that: (a) queries MLflow for the most recent metric timestamp (b) returns StallResult(stale=True/False, last_metric_ts, minutes_since) (c) handles missing run_id gracefully (returns stale=False) (d) handles MLflow server unavailability gracefully (returns stale=False with warning logged) Test with mocked MLflow client.

task.title: Add MLFLOW_STALL_THRESHOLD_MINUTES to .env.example
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test .env.example contains MLFLOW_STALL_THRESHOLD_MINUTES with default 15.

task.title: Wire stall detection into GpuHeartbeatMonitor
task.tdd-spec: RED: Test that GpuHeartbeatMonitor optionally accepts an MLflow run_id and checks for metric staleness on each heartbeat tick. When stale, log ERROR + tag the run with "stall_detected=true" via MlflowClient. Test with mocked MLflow client.

phase.success-criteria: - MLflow metric staleness detected within 1 heartbeat cycle after threshold - Stale runs tagged with "stall_detected=true" for easy filtering - Graceful when MLflow unavailable (advisory, not blocking) - All tests in make test-staging with mocked MLflow

execution-order.note: Phases are designed for sequential execution but each is independently mergeable. Phase 1 is the highest priority (prevents the 4-hour incident). Phases 2-3 provide the monitoring foundation. Phases 4-8 build on it.
execution-order.dependency-graph: Phase 1 (CUDA guard) → independent, merge first Phase 2 (GPU heartbeat) → independent of Phase 1 Phase 3 (epoch progress) → independent of Phase 1 Phase 4 (stall detection) → depends on Phase 2 + 3 (reads heartbeat.json) Phase 5 (Prefect hooks) → depends on Phase 3 (uses StructuredLogger) Phase 6 (Docker healthcheck) → depends on Phase 4 (uses healthcheck script) Phase 7 (Prometheus gauges) → depends on Phase 2 (wires into heartbeat) Phase 8 (MLflow stall alert) → depends on Phase 2 (wires into heartbeat)
execution-order.parallelism: Phases 1, 2, 3 can be implemented in parallel (no dependencies). Phases 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 must follow their dependencies. Recommended: implement 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 → 8 (linear for self-learning-iterative-coder, but parallelizable if needed)

coverage-matrix.item: require_cuda_context() fails immediately when CUDA unavailable
GpuHeartbeatMonitor detects GPU util < 5% for > 2 min
PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 in all 3 base Dockerfiles (Dockerfile.base:166, Dockerfile.base-cpu:85, Dockerfile.base-light:85). Phase 3 adds explicit sys.stdout.flush() as defense-in-depth.
StructuredEventLogger emits JSONL + heartbeat after every epoch
Prefect task hooks emit start/end/duration for every @task
Prometheus gauges for CPU/GPU/memory visible in Grafana
JSONL format via StructuredEventLogger (extends existing infrastructure)
